paraphrase: The Long Night was a legendary apocalyptic era thousands of years ago when winter lasted a generation, there was no daylight, and Others riding dead horses and monstrous spiders came from the North to destroy all living things.
The Long Night affected not just Westeros but also the Rhoyne and the Further East, suggesting it was a worldwide catastrophe.
Melisandre says the Long Night is returning, bringing an apocalyptic 'night that never ends.'
Melisandre says the only hope against the returning Long Night is a hero named Azor Ahai.
The Northerners' legend of the 'last hero' says he joined with the children of the forest and won the Battle for the Dawn against the Others.
Descendants of the Rhoynar say a hero convinced their gods to bring back the day with a secret song.
The Yi Tish say a woman with a monkey's tail restored the sun.

The Asshai'i name the hero Azor Ahai and prophesy their rebirth to save the world again; other cultures use the names Hyrkoon the Hero, Yin Tar, Neferion, Eldric Shadowchaser, and 'the prince that was promised.'
Some people think 'the prince that was promised' and Azor Ahai are separate figures.

Melisandre, Aemon Targaryen, and George R. R. Martin himself all talk as though Azor Ahai and 'the prince that was promised' are the same thing, and no one in the books has ever distinguished between the two.
The 'stallion who mounts the world' prophecy may also be connected to the same figure -- he too is described as a 'prince' and is promised in prophecy.
Melisandre says Azor Ahai is prophesied to be born beneath a bleeding star, amidst salt and smoke.
Aemon Targaryen, Benerro (the High Red Priest of Volantis), and Marwyn the Mage (who says he doesn't trust prophecy) all independently cite the same star/salt/smoke signs.
Rhaegar Targaryen believed in these prophetic signs, and it seems likely his wife Elia and other family members did as well.
According to Melisandre and Aemon, another sign is a red or burning sword called Lightbringer, which also appears in several legends.
Sam Tarly suggests the sword may simply be an ordinary Valyrian steel sword, not a uniquely magical one.
Melisandre says Azor Ahai will draw the sword from fire.
One legend says the creation of the original Lightbringer required the sacrificial execution of Azor Ahai's wife, Nissa Nissa.
Davos speculates Azor Ahai reborn might need to make a similar sacrifice, but that is never actually stated to be part of any prophecy, so it might not be important.

Melisandre, Aemon, and Benerro all say Azor Ahai has something to do with dragons; Melisandre specifically says Azor Ahai is prophesied 'to wake dragons from stone,' which apparently requires sacrificing someone with king's blood.

Since Melisandre is the only one claiming the specific 'stone dragons require a king's-blood sacrifice' detail, and she admits she sometimes misinterprets prophecy, it's possible she is wrong about that specific detail -- though all sources agree Azor Ahai is generally associated with dragons.
Aemon implies Azor Ahai must have Targaryen blood, and the Ghost of High Heart specifies it must be the line of Aerys and Rhaella specifically.

spoken_quote: the prince that was promised would be born of their line
paraphrase: Ser Barristan says Aerys and Rhaella Targaryen married because a woods witch said 'the prince that was promised would be born of their line.'
window: was promised would be born of their line
verbatim: “Your grandsire commanded it. A woods witch had told him that the prince was promised would be born of their line.”

paraphrase: Barristan's description of the woods witch matches the description of the Ghost of High Heart -- both are very short women who give prophecies, both are associated with the children of the forest, and both were close to someone named Jenny -- so they're almost certainly the same person.

The Ghost of High Heart has proven the reliability of her prophecies with a lot of impressively accurate predictions, so her claim that Azor Ahai must have the blood of Aerys and Rhaella is likely correct.

spoken_quote: the dragon must have three heads
paraphrase: Both Aemon and Rhaegar, in a vision, say 'the dragon must have three heads.'
verbatim: That had been one of his last good days. After that the old man spent more time sleeping than awake, curled up beneath a pile of furs in the captain’s cabin. Sometimes he would mutter in his sleep. When he woke he’d call for Sam, insisting that he had to tell him something, but oft as not he would have forgotten what he meant to say by the time that Sam arrived. Even when he did recall, his talk was all a jumble. He spoke of dreams and never named the dreamer, of a glass candle that could not be lit and eggs that would not hatch. He said the sphinx was the riddle, not the riddler, whatever that meant. He asked Sam to read for him from a book by Septon Barth, whose writings had been burned during the reign of Baelor the Blessed. Once he woke up weeping. “The dragon must have three heads,” he wailed, “but I am too old and frail to be one of them. I should be with her, showing her the way, but my body has betrayed me.”

paraphrase: Some people think the three heads of the dragon means Azor Ahai is actually three separate people, but the video says that isn't what the text suggests.
What the three heads of the dragon seems to actually mean is that Azor Ahai should have two companions who help them out, maybe riding dragons alongside them.
Aemon says he himself could have been one of the three heads if he weren't so old, implying the three heads are not specially prophesied figures but could be any Targaryen, or anyone at all.

spoken_quote: His is the song of ice and fire
paraphrase: Rhaegar, in a vision, says of 'the prince that was promised' that 'His is the song of ice and fire' -- vague and not clearly part of any formal prophecy, but the video says it's probably worth keeping in mind.
Melisandre says Stannis Baratheon is Azor Ahai and tries very hard to make him fit the seven signs.
Like every character alive during A Clash of Kings, Stannis is around to see the red comet in the sky, which Dany and the Dothraki name the 'Bleeding Star.'

Not long after the comet appears, at the ceremony at Dragonstone where statues of the Seven are burned, Stannis is proclaimed Azor Ahai -- a symbolic rebirth happening shortly after (if not under) a bleeding star, amid salt (the ocean) and smoke (the burning gods).
At the same ceremony, Stannis draws a sword from the fire and names it Lightbringer.
Jon points out that the sword is not warm or hot, as Lightbringer should be.
Aemon says 'the sword is wrong,' and describes its light as an 'empty glamor' -- perhaps whatever magic it has is just another of Melisandre's illusions.
Sallador Saan says 'That sword was not Lightbringer.'
verbatim: “I have attended to it, good ser. Though His Grace frowns so whenever he does see me that I tremble to come before him. Do you think he would like me better if I wore a hair shirt and never smiled? Well, I will not do it. I am an honest man, he must suffer me in silk and samite. Or else I shall take my ships where I am better loved. That sword was not Lightbringer, my friend.”

paraphrase: Melisandre tries to get Stannis to sacrifice someone with king's blood to awaken stone dragons; so far that hasn't happened -- no sacrifice, no dragons.
Stannis does have some Targaryen blood, but not from the Aerys-and-Rhaella line specifically, so he fails the blood sign too.
Stannis has no apparent connection to 'the song of ice and fire' or the three heads of the dragon, unless you stretch it to argue Stannis, Melisandre, and Davos are the three heads.

Narratively, Stannis is a harsh, bitter, unromantic man, not a 'save-the-world-with-a-magic-sword' kind of guy, and says as much himself -- and it's ironic that a true champion of R'hllor would accidentally set himself on fire with his own supposed Lightbringer.

The fact that the story leads readers to believe Stannis is Azor Ahai is itself a reason to doubt it -- it would be poor storytelling for GRRM to reveal the answer to this central mystery at the very start of the second book, and GRRM has said prophecies shouldn't be too literal and too easy.
Melisandre has tried and failed to make Stannis literally fulfill the Azor Ahai prophecy; Aemon says she has misread the signs and is deceiving herself because she wants to believe.
Aemon and Benerro both say Daenerys Targaryen is Azor Ahai.

In the final chapter of Book 1, Dany sees the Bleeding Star appear, walks into the flames, and amid smoke is reborn as the mother of dragons -- 'stone dragons' she wakes from eggs -- which perfectly fits the star, smoke, and stone-dragon signs.
The salt sign for Dany probably comes from salty, smoky Dragonstone where she was literally born, if not from tears or sweat at the pyre.
Dany's dragons could be her version of Lightbringer, a fiery weapon -- supported by Xaro Xhoan Daxos describing Dany's dragons as a 'flaming sword.'
Aemon says translation errors are a problem with the prophecy -- the idea that Lightbringer is a literal sword might be one such mistranslation, supporting the dragons-as-Lightbringer reading.

The awakening of the stone dragons (creation of Dany's 'burning sword') required two sacrifices -- of her husband Khal Drogo, and of her stillborn son Rhaego, who carried Targaryen king's blood through Dany.

spoken_quote: Only death can pay for life
paraphrase: Dany herself says, in the same chapter, 'Only death can pay for life,' tying these deaths to the birth of her dragons.

This satisfies both kinds of sacrifice required by the prophecy: sacrifice of a loved one (paralleling Nissa Nissa) for the burning sword, and sacrifice of king's blood to awaken stone dragons, as Melisandre's prophecy specifies.
Daenerys is the daughter of Aerys and Rhaella Targaryen, so she has the required blood.
The three heads of the dragon might relate to Dany's three dragons, or perhaps to three future dragon-riders.
Dany has no clear connection to 'the song of ice and fire,' though the video notes that phrase and the three-heads sign are vague and not actually part of any formal prophecy anyway.

Narratively, Dany's many titles and epithets (Stormborn, the Unburnt, Breaker of Chains, Mother of Dragons, Queen of Meereen, etc.) and the fact that GRRM has hyped her up throughout the series support her being the prophesied savior.
The last we saw of Jon Snow, he had been stabbed four times, but he could be resurrected by Melisandre -- red priests can do this, as shown by the precedents of Beric Dondarrion and Thoros of Myr.
There's evidence Jon might warg into his direwolf Ghost before returning to his own body -- wargs can do this, and Jon is shown to be unknowingly a powerful warg in the A Dance with Dragons Prologue.
This warg idea is supported by a vision Melisandre has of Jon in which he changes from a man, to a wolf, and back to a man again.
Jon's last word after being stabbed is 'Ghost.'

At the stabbing scene, several signs cluster together: Jon's wound is smoking, Bowen Marsh is crying salty tears, and Ser Patrick -- whose heraldry features stars -- bleeds heavily; the video reads this as the star, the smoke, and the salt appearing right at Jon's death.

spoken_quote: promised prince, born in smoke and salt
paraphrase: Jon brings up the 'promised prince, born in smoke and salt' in the very same chapter as his stabbing.
verbatim: “A grey girl on a dying horse. Daggers in the dark. A promised prince, born in smoke and salt. It seems to me that you make nothing but mistakes, my lady. Where is Stannis? What of Rattleshirt and his spearwives? Where is my sister?”

paraphrase: The video acknowledges the star sign is 'a bit dodgy' for Jon, but asks whether this cluster of signs appearing right at his death can really be a coincidence.
Jon does have a Valyrian steel sword (Longclaw), but the video calls it 'hardly Lightbringer.'

spoken_quote: I am the sword in the darkness
paraphrase: It's arguable that the Night's Watch itself is Jon's fiery weapon -- their vow includes 'I am the sword in the darkness,' they are literally a weapon against the Others, and Jon sacrificed his love Ygritte for the Watch.
Alternatively, the sign of the sword may be a mistranslation or misinterpretation of the sign of the dragons, in which case Jon might need to acquire one of Dany's dragons.
Jon might 'wake a dragon from stone' by discovering his Targaryen parentage in the Winterfell crypts -- there are theories connecting this to Rhaegar's harp.
Another idea raised is that Jon might wake a dragon from within the Wall itself.
Both the crypts and in-the-Wall dragon ideas are just future speculation -- for now Jon has no dragon, probably no sword, and probably no sacrifice, though these could still happen in later books.

If R+L=J is true, which the video says is 'very probably' the case, then Jon has the required Targaryen blood, which would also literally make him 'the prince that was promised' and connect him to 'ice and fire.'
Melisandre says that whenever she tries to see Azor Ahai in her fire, R'hllor shows her Snow -- Jon Snow -- and this happens repeatedly.
Jon has a dream in which he wields a burning red sword against the Others at the Wall, shouts about fire, and even slays the woman he loves with the red sword.
Aegon, if he really is who he claims, has the Targaryen blood and apparently the star sign too.
Rhaegar Targaryen also had the blood and was born amid salt and smoke.
Victarion has a burned, smoking hand.
Davos was reborn amidst salt and smoke.
The Lightning Lord (Beric Dondarrion) has a burning sword.
Brienne has a red sword.
Tyrion would make 'a nice trio' with Dany and Jon under the three-heads-of-the-dragon idea.
Ramsay shares the surname 'Snow' with Jon.

The video uses several deliberately absurd examples (e.g., Jaime as a 'Nissa Nissa' for Cersei, Hot Pie's salty pies baked in smoky ovens) to argue that you can manufacture tenuous Azor Ahai 'connections' for almost any character, and that only Dany and Jon are actually backed by strong, repeated evidence.
It would be bad storytelling for the series to spend the whole story building up Dany and Jon only to reveal a minor character like Aegon or Ramsay as the true savior instead.
It would be even worse storytelling than a third candidate to build up all this foreshadowing, all these visions and hints, and then throw them away by saying they mean nothing.

Azor Ahai doesn't have to be a perfect fairy-tale hero who fixes everything -- prophecy is complicated and messy in this series, but given all the accumulated evidence, the video argues Azor Ahai 'has to mean something.'

Taken together, the evidence currently points to two people -- Jon and Dany -- with Dany fitting the prophecy almost perfectly and Jon fitting most of it plus extra hints; together they represent 'ice and fire,' the title of the series.
